title: Your First Song: Rhythm, Melody, and the Mathematics of Music
description: Experience rhythm, melody, and harmony through active participation. This session works with or without an instrument -- body percussion and humming are all you need.

steps.instruction: Read about rhythm and meter. Clap a steady beat at a comfortable tempo. Now clap a simple rhythmic pattern on top of the beat: quarter-quarter-two eighths-quarter (ONE TWO one-and THREE). This is the clave pattern at the heart of Afro-Cuban music and jazz. Feel the difference between the steady pulse and the rhythmic pattern.
steps.expectedOutcome: You can maintain a steady beat while clapping a separate rhythmic pattern on top -- the foundation of all rhythmic musicianship
steps.hint: Use your dominant hand for the rhythmic pattern and your non-dominant hand as the steady beat -- or use your foot for the steady pulse

steps.instruction: Read about scales and intervals. Hum or sing the major scale (do-re-mi-fa-sol-la-ti-do). Notice the two half-steps between mi-fa and ti-do -- these create the scale's characteristic bright sound. Now hum the pentatonic scale: do-re-mi-sol-la. Notice how much smoother and less tense it feels without the half-steps.
steps.expectedOutcome: You can hear and feel the difference between major and pentatonic scales, understanding how the half-step creates the major scale's distinctive tension and resolution
steps.hint: The pentatonic scale is the black keys on a piano -- play any combination of only black keys and it will always sound good

steps.instruction: Read about harmony and the I-IV-V progression. If you have an instrument, play or have someone play C major (C-E-G), F major (F-A-C), and G major (G-B-D). If not, listen to a 12-bar blues. Notice how the chord changes create tension (at G) and resolution (returning to C). This movement of tension and release is what drives tonal music.
steps.expectedOutcome: You can hear harmonic tension and resolution and understand the basic I-IV-V progression that underlies enormous amounts of Western music
steps.hint: Hum along with each chord -- your voice will naturally want to stay within the chord's notes

steps.instruction: Read about acoustics of instruments. If you have any instrument available, explore how you can change pitch: on a guitar, shorter string = higher pitch; on a wind instrument, opening holes shortens the air column. If no instrument, explore your voice: sing and change the shape of your mouth and throat -- what happens to pitch and tone?
steps.expectedOutcome: You understand the physical relationship between the vibrating object (string, air column, vocal cord) and the pitch it produces

steps.instruction: Read about music history. Listen to two short examples: a Bach fugue (Baroque counterpoint) and a Charlie Parker bebop jazz piece. What is similar? What is different? How does the historical context (18th century Germany vs. 1940s New York) shape the music?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can hear the connection between jazz and classical counterpoint and articulate how historical and cultural context shapes musical style
